text.title: Modular Monolith Core Principles
text.summary: Five non-negotiable rules that define a modular monolith: bounded context per module, public API only, no shared domain models, data isolation, no cross-schema joins.

rule.statement: Each module must represent exactly one bounded context — a distinct business domain with its own vocabulary, data ownership, and lifecycle.
rule.rationale: Modules named after technical layers (utils, helpers, common) are not modules; they are coupling points. Modules named after business domains (Orders, Payments, Inventory) enforce team ownership and enable future extraction.

example.code: src/ users/ # Authentication, profiles orders/ # Cart, checkout, order lifecycle payments/ # Transactions, refunds inventory/ # Stock, warehouses notifications/ # Email, SMS, push

antipattern.description: Module named after technical role, not business domain
antipattern.reason: utils/, helpers/, common/ become dumping grounds for cross-module logic, recreating tight coupling

rule.statement: Modules must communicate exclusively through their declared public API. Direct imports of internal classes, models, or functions from another module are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Bypassing public APIs creates invisible coupling. When module internals change, all callers break. The public API is the contract — it is the only stable interface.

example.code: # WRONG: direct import of internal model from orders.models import Order order = Order.objects.get(id=123)
# RIGHT: through public service API from orders.api import OrderService order = OrderService.get_order(123)

rule.statement: Each module owns its domain models. When module A needs data from module B, it uses a DTO (Data Transfer Object) returned by B's public API — never B's internal model class.
rule.rationale: Shared domain models create hidden coupling: a change to the shared model forces changes across all consumers. DTOs are immutable value objects with no business logic; they decouple the wire format from the internal model.

example.code: # orders/models.py — Orders' internal view of a customer class OrderCustomer: user_id: int # Reference by ID only name: str # Denormalized for display — NOT the User model

rule.statement: Each module manages its own database schema or database instance. No module may read from or write to tables owned by another module.
rule.rationale: Shared tables create the same coupling as shared code. A schema migration in one module should never break another. Data ownership is the foundation of independent module evolution.

rule.statement: Never write SQL that joins tables across module schemas. Query data through module APIs; denormalize when read performance requires it.
rule.rationale: Cross-schema joins are invisible boundary violations. They bypass the module's public API, expose internal schema structure, and survive code review invisibly. They also block independent schema migration.

example.code: -- WRONG: cross-schema join bypasses module boundary SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users.accounts u JOIN orders.orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

example.note: Denormalize the customer name into the orders schema at write time, or query via API and join in application code
